summary: Gruber examines Bloomberg Businessweek's explosive report alleging that Chinese intelligence compromised thousands of data center servers by inserting rogue chips into Supermicro motherboards used by Apple, Amazon, and others. He highlights the stark contradiction between Bloomberg's sourcing from senior national security officials and the vehement, detailed denials from both Apple and Amazon. Gruber argues that someone must be wrong or lying, as these positions cannot coexist. He finds the denials credible, particularly Amazon CISO Steve Schmidt's signed response and Apple PR's track record of not lying. While acknowledging the broader thesis about supply chain vulnerability rings true, he notes a technical inconsistency in how air-gapped servers could phone home to China. He concludes that significant credibility damage awaits either Bloomberg or the two most valuable companies in the world.
key_points: Bloomberg's sourcing (six current and former senior national security officials) directly contradicts Apple's and Amazon's categorical, on-the-record denials — one side must be wrong or lying
Apple PR doesn't lie in Gruber's experience — they spin, but understand credibility is a key company asset, and would say nothing rather than issue a false statement
Amazon's denial carries extra weight because it was personally signed by CISO Steve Schmidt, who would be most familiar with any such breach
The broader supply chain security thesis is credible — the tech industry's dependence on Chinese manufacturing creates a 'Satan's bargain' between supply and security
A technical inconsistency undermines the story: if compromised servers weren't exposed to the public internet, how would rogue chips phone home to Chinese servers?
money_quotes: Someone is either wrong or lying. This cannot all be true.
I see no way around it: either Bloomberg's report is significantly wrong, at least as pertains to Amazon and Apple, or Apple and Amazon have issued blatantly false denials.
But they don't lie, because they understand that one of Apple's key assets is its credibility. They'd say nothing before they'd lie.
One way or the other, there is more to come on this story, and the credibility of either Bloomberg, or Apple and Amazon, is going to take a significant hit.
Technical details aside, the whole central thesis of the story rings true — China cannot be trusted as a state actor, but the entire technology industry is dependent upon the Chinese supply chain.

You end up with a classic Satan's bargain. You can have less supply than you want and guarantee it's secure, or you can have the supply you need, but there will be risk. Every organization has accepted the second proposition.
I do not understand how, if these servers are not exposed to the public internet, they could 'phone home' to Chinese servers outside the data centers.
Two of Elemental's biggest early clients were the Mormon church, which used the technology to beam sermons to congregations around the world, and the adult film industry, which did not.
key_insight: The irreconcilable contradiction between Bloomberg's sourcing and Apple/Amazon's categorical denials means one side's credibility will be destroyed, but the underlying supply chain security vulnerability is real regardless.
